coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and selective dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence detection of dopamine based on carbon dots-gold nanoclusters hybrid
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yu-Sheng
Pan, Chang-Gang
Cao, Hai-Xia
Yue, Min-Zhi
Wang, Li
Liang, Guo-Xi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based ratiometric fluorescent probe, carbon dots-gold nanoclusters hybrid (CDots-AuNCs), was fabricated for selective, sensitive and reliable sensing of dopamine (DA). This FRET probe is comprised of a two-fluorophore, where carbon dots (CDots) serve as the energy donor and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) as the acceptor, with dual emission peaks at 420 nm and 610 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The addition of DA to this probe solution resulted in the fluorescence at 610 nm quenching, while the blue fluorescence at 420 nm recovering. By monitoring the change of ratiometric fluorescent intensity at 420 and 610 nm, the DA could be detected with the range from 5 to 180 nM and a limit of detection around 2.9 nM. Finally, the developed sensing method was successfully applied to DA determination in serum samples with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 95%–105%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon dots
Au nanoclusters
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of DA in human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Nature Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20140577 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053 ) and Advanced Talents Science Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG052 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the CDots-AuNCs RF assay of DA.
XPS spectra of the CDots. (a) Full scan spectrum, (b)–(d) High-resolution peaks of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.
TEM images of the as-synthesized (a) AuNCs, (b) CDots, (c) CDots-AuNCs nanohybrid. (Inserts are high-resolution TEM images).
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) UV–vis spectrum of AuNCs, CDots and CDots-AuNCs hybrid, (c) XRD pattern of CDots-AuNCs hybrid.

(a) Emission spectrum of the CDots and excitation spectrum of AuNCs, (b) Fluorescent spectra of AuNCs, CDots, mixture of CDots and AuNCs, and CDots-AuNCs hybrid. Inset: The corresponding photographs under UV lamp (λex = 365 nm, from left to right are AuNCs, CDots-AuNCs, CDots, respectively).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDots-AuNCs hybrid system upon the exposure to different concentrations of DA (inset: regionally enlarged drawing), (b) The linearity of the system towards different concentrations of DA (inset: the corresponding photographs under UV lamp, λex = 365 nm). The mixtures were kept for 30 min at room temperature before the fluorescence intensity of the probe solution was measured. Excitation at 380 nm.

The selectivity of the RF sensor to various molecules: DA (150 nM); other analytes (100 μM). (DA, dopamine; Ala, alanine; Arg, arginine; Asp, aspartic acid; Cys, cysteine; Gln, glutamine; Glu, glutamic acid; His, histidine; Ile, isoleucine; Leu, leucine; Lys, lysine; Trp, tryptophan; Tyr, tyrosine; AA, ascorbic acid; UA, uric acid; Glc, glucose; Ins, Inositol; ACH, acetylcholine; GABA; serotonin; and NE, norephinephrine). The mixtures were kept for 30 min at room temperature before the fluorescence intensity of the probe solution was measured. Excitation at 380 nm.
Recoveries of DA in spiked human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and selective dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence detection of dopamine based on carbon dots-gold nanoclusters hybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environment Science, School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive and selective dopamine detection based on carbon dots-gold nanoclusters hybrid

In this work, a novel fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based ratiometric fluorescent probe, carbon dots-gold nanoclusters hybrid (CDots-AuNCs), was fabricated for selective, sensitive and reliable sensing of dopamine (DA). This FRET probe is comprised of a two-fluorophore, where carbon dots (CDots) serve as the energy donor and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) as the acceptor, with dual emission peaks at 420 nm and 610 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The addition of DA to this probe solution resulted in the fluorescence at 610 nm quenching, while the blue fluorescence at 420 nm recovering. By monitoring the change of ratiometric fluorescent intensity at 420 and 610 nm, the DA could be detected with the range from 5 to 180 nM and a limit of detection around 2.9 nM. Finally, the developed sensing method was successfully applied to DA determination in serum samples with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 95%–105%.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is an important hormone and neurotransmitter in the catecholamine and phenethylamine families. It plays a number of significant roles in the human brain and body and controls many biological functions such as motivation, emotion, endocrine regulation, and locomotion [1,2]. Many diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and anorexia are associated with abnormal DA levels [3]. However, the concentration of DA in living system ranges from 0.1 mM to 1.0 mM, which is very low [4,5]. Therefore, sensitive detection of DA is still challenging.

Up to date, many techniques have been developed for the detection of DA including high performance liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry, spectrophotometry, and electrochemical techniques [6–9]. However, these techniques have a lot of drawbacks. For example, the chromatographic and mass spectrometric approaches rely on expensive equipment and specific sample pretreatment procedures [10,11]; the spectrophotometric method has relatively low sensitivity [12]; electrochemical method often suffers from the interferences of ascorbic acid owing to the similar oxidation potential [13].

Fluorescent method is a better alternative than those mentioned above due to its outstanding features such as low cost, simple, high sensitivity, practicability and good selectivity. Moreover, it has the advantage of detecting the DA in complex biological matrixes without the separation procedure [14]. Recently, several interesting fluorescent probes such as organic dyes and single-walled carbon nanotubes have been developed to selectively, sensitively and directly detect neurotransmitters, including DA [15–18]. However, currently, fluorescence-based probes for DA are mainly single wavelength intensity modulation type sensors.

As we all know, the emission intensity may be influenced by many factors, such as the drifts of the optoelectronic system (lamps and detectors), the probe concentration, autofluorescence in complicated biosystems, which are prone to disturbance in quantitative detection [19,20]. Ratiometric fluorescence (RF) sensors can avoid these problems by measure the ratio of two emission intensities at different wavelengths [21]. Moreover, this sort of sensors have been demonstrated as visual sensing platforms and widely applied for the detection of various targets such as ions, protein, DNA, and so on [22–25]. Fluorescence resonance energy-transfer (FRET) is a commonly used sensing mechanism in the design of RF probes, in which there are two fluorophores, one act as a donor and another act as an acceptor, excited donor energy is transferred to an acceptor without any photon-emission. FRET-based RF probes, calculated the intensities at two different emission wavelengths, provide a built-in correction for environmental effects and can also increase the dynamic range of fluorescence measurement [26]. In particular, FRET has the advantage of remarkable emission shifts with fixed excitation wavelengths [27]. For FRET-based RF sensors, the design and development of a donor-acceptor pair is very critical.

In the merits of large Stokes shift, long lifetime, biocompatibility, ease of conjugation, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) have popularized as a promising class of fluorescence materials for biosensing and imaging [28]. On the other hand, carbon dots (CDots) have attracted much attention due to their low cost, green synthesis, good biosafety, and environmental friendliness [29]. Especially, their outstanding photoluminescence properties, such as a lack of optical blinking and low photobleaching, make them perfect substitutes of traditional fluorophores as fluorescence donors and have been widely used in sensing fields [30]. Several groups have recently described CDots-AuNCs-based probes for RF sensing chemicals [31–34]. However, their application is still scarce. Additionally, most of them are not based FRET strategy. Seeking suitable fluorescence CDots and AuNCs to construct FRET-based RF sensors and expanding their applications are still of interest.

Herein, we proposed an effective FRET-based RF sensor for the detection of DA by combining CDots and AuNCs to form CDots-AuNCs hybrid. Scheme 1 schematically illustrates the principle of sensing. In this sensor, CDots act as an energy donor, AuNCs as an acceptor. The latter fluorescence can be quenched by DA, resulting in the emission from CDots recovering. Consequently, the ratio of two fluorescence intensities gradually changed corresponding to the concentrations of DA, which could be easily observed by the naked eyes under an UV lamp, resulting in visualization detection. This sensor demonstrates several advantages: First, since it is a RF probe toward DA, the influence of environmental effects can be avoided, which can ensure more accurate detection. Second, it is a highly sensitive and selective method with a very low detection limit of 2.9 nM DA. Moreover, the toxicity of both donor and acceptor is very low, which benefit the bioanalysis more desirable.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA, fraction V) was purchased from Sangon (Shanghai, China). 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-carbodii-mide (EDC) and N-hydroxy-sulfosuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). L-dopamine hydrochloride (DA), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), l-cysteine (l-Cys), citric acid (CA), and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

All other reagents were of analytical grade. Ultra-pure water was used in all experiment. Unless otherwise stated, the detection solution was 10 mM pH = 9.0 phosphate buffer solution (PBS 10 mM, Na2HPO4, 2 mM KH2PO4, 137 mM NaCl, 2.7 mM KCl).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of CDots were performed on a ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific). The morphology of CDots, AuNCs, CDots-AuNCs were analyzed using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL). The mean particle sizes of the nanoparticles were analyzed using a ZetaPALS laser particle size analyzer (Brookhaven). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker). FT-IR spectra were recorded on Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Nicolet). All fluorescence spectra were measured on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-3600 UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu).

The CDots were prepared by a hydrothermal method [35]. Briefly, 2 g CA and 1 g l-cysteine were sufficiently dissolved in 5 mL water. The mixed solution was evaporated at 70 °C until dry. Then, the resulted thick syrup was transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. The obtained black syrup product was dispersed in 100 mL ultrapure water and neutralized with 1 M NaOH solution. After centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min, the optically transparent sample was further dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for purification. The purified CDots were preserved at 4 °C for further use.

The BSA-stabilized AuNCs were synthesized according to a previously reported method with minor modification [36]. Briefly, 5 mL HAuCl4 (10 mM) and 5 mL BSA (50 mg/mL) were mixed and stirred at 800 rpm in the dark for 30 min. After added 500 μL NaOH (1 M), the mixture was heated at 50 °C for 7 h under vigorous stirring. After centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min, the transparent sample was further dialyzed with membrane (1000 Da) for purification. Finally, the AuNCs powder was obtained by freeze drying the solution.

The AuNCs were subsequently conjugated to CDots as follows: 250 μL EDC/NHS aqueous solution (10 mg/mL for each) was added to 3 mL AuNCs solution (1.0 mg/mL) to activate the carboxyl group. Then, 500 μL of CDots solution (0.2 mg/mL) was added to the above mentioned AuNCs solution and incubated in the dark with constant stirring for 2 h. The CDots-AuNCs hybrid was dialyzed for 24 h with a dialysis membrane (100,000 MWCO). The resulting CDots-AuNCs hybrid was stored at 4 °C for future use.

Aliquots of 50 μL freshly prepared CDots-AuNCs hybrid were mixed with 50 μL different concentrations (0–180 nM) of DA solutions. Then the solution was diluted to 1 mL with PBS solution (pH 9) and incubated for 30 min at room temperature. Upon excitation at 380 nm, the fluorescence was recorded from 400 nm to 700 nm, and the slit widths were 5 nm for both excitation and emission. All measurements were carried out in triplicate for accurate calculations to develop a standard protocol.

Firstly, XPS was employed to characterize the surface composition and chemical state of CDots. The full scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 1 a) indicated that the sample consisted of O, C, N and S elements. Three peaks located at 531, 400, and 285 eV corresponded to O1s, N1s and C1s, respectively, which could be discerned clearly. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s exhibited three wide main peaks centered at 285, 286 and 288 eV, which were respectively attributed to CC, CO/CN/CS, and CO bonds (Fig. 1b) [35]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. 1c) revealed the presence of pyridinic type N (399.7 eV), pyrrolic type (400.6 eV) N [35]. The peak at ∼531 eV of O1s (Fig. 1d) was assigned to CO/CO bonds [36]. The XPS data confirmed the presence of COOH and NH2 functional groups in the surface of CDots, which could conjugate with NH2 and COOH of BSA-stabilized AuNCs through EDC/NHS reaction.

The morphology and size of the AuNCs, CDots, and CDots-AuNCs nanohybrid were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 , both AuNCs and CDots were all spherical in shape and well dispersed in water, the average size was about 3 nm and 4 nm for AuNCs and CDots, respectively. The high-resolution TEM image of AuNCs (inserts in Fig. 2a) indicated that it possessed a crystalline structure of parallel crystal planes of lattice spacing 0.21 nm, which agreed well with the Au (111) facet. The high-resolution TEM image of CDots showed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inserts in Fig. 2b), which is close to the (100) facet of graphite carbon [36,37]. Notably, compared with the individual CDots and AuNCs, CDots-AuNCs nanohybrid showed a very large size (∼40 nm) because of many CDot-AuNC conjugates formed (Fig. 2c).
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis at room temperature also showed that the average hydrodynamic diameters of AuNCs, CDots, and CDots-AuNCs were about 2.4, 5 and 40 nm, respectively (Fig. S1).

Subsequently, CDots-AuNCs hybrid was characterized by FT-IR, UV–vis and XRD patterns to identify the hybrid formation. Fig. 3 a depicted the FT-IR spectrum of AuNCs, CDots and CDots-AuNCs hybrid, respectively. The AuNCs showed stretching vibrations of OH at 3410 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups at 1652 and 1540 cm−1. CDots have many characteristic peaks, such as OH (3468 cm−1), NH (3383–3193 cm−1), CH (3031 cm−1), CN (1646 cm−1), CC (1597 cm−1), CN (1396 and 1250 cm−1), and CO (1090 cm−1). For the CDots-AuNCs hybrid, a broad absorption band at 3410 cm−1 and narrow absorption bands at 1652 and 1540 cm−1 were assigned to AuNCs, the characteristic peak at 1250 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of the CN groups and 1090 cm−1 was related to CO stretching vibration from the CDots. The results revealed that the CDots-AuNCs hybrid showed signals of both CDots and AuNCs.

Fig. 3b showed the UV–vis absorption change of the CDots, AuNCs and CDots-AuNCs hybrid. Pure CDots had a broad absorption from 300 nm to 400 nm. Considerable UV–vis absorbance peak was not observed for AuNCs. However, for CDots-AuNCs hybrid, a peak at about from 260 nm and a broad peak at 300–400 nm were exhibited.

The XRD pattern of CDots-AuNCs hybrid (Fig. 3c) exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered at about 25°, which further confirmed the graphene structure of the CDots (JCPDS No. 01-0646); the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 38.2° and 44.9°, which corresponded to the (111), (200) crystalline planes of metallic Au (JCPDS No. 04-0783).

Under 380 nm excitation, an FRET process between CDots and AuNCs could occur due to the emissions of the donor (CDots) was absorbed by the acceptor (AuNCs). To study the mechanism, fluorescence properties of CDots, AuNCs were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence excitation spectra of AuNCs were overlapped with the emission spectra of the CDots in 400–500 nm, which confirmed that FRET could occur between them. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDots excited with 380 nm UV light is calculated to be ∼68.0% by selecting the quinine sulfate as standard, QY of AuNCs is ∼8% by measured at 420 nm UV light with rhodamine 6G as a standard, indicating that the donor-acceptor could provide strong energy-transfer.

FRET occurs when the distance between a donor and an acceptor is within 10 nm [38]. In our proposal, the donor (CDots) and acceptor (AuNCs) were conjugated together via EDC/NHS reaction. After formation of the CDots-AuNCs hybrid, the distance between CDots and AuNCs was getting close enough to perform FRET (˂2 nm according to the high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 2c).

As a result, as shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of CDots at 420 nm was obviously quenched about 30% when formation hybrid due to FRET, whereas the emission of CDots remained constant when only mix CDots and AuNCs solution together.

The basic principle of CDots-AuNCs hybrid-DA detection is illustrated in Scheme 1. The negative charges, carboxyl groups on the surface of the AuNCs enable noncovalent interaction with positive charge, amine functional groups, and phenyl in DA through electrostaticinte interactions, π-π stacking, and hydrogen bonding. The DA molecules adsorbed onto the surface of AuNCs were oxidized to o-quinone in air-containing solution, and o-quinone could accept electrons from the photo-excited AuNCs with consequent AuNCs fluorescence quenching [39]. While no fluorescence quenching occured in the case of single CDots. Different from individual AuNCs, CDots, with increasing the concentration of DA, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs from CDots-AuNCs nanohybrid at 610 nm shows continuous decreasing, whereas that of blue emission from the conjugated CDots shows continuous increasing.

The pH value and reaction time, which may greatly influence the performance of fluorescence sensors, were studied. The results were presented in Fig. S2, in that the RF response (I420/I610) toward DA was obviously pH and reaction time dependent. As can be seen, the RF intensities in alkali conditions (pH 9.0 and 11) were much higher than that in acidic (pH 5) and neutral (pH 7) conditions; a faster sensing response was obtained in weak alkali conditions (pH 9.0) than in strong alkali conditions (pH 11.0). Moreover, it has been reported that DA could produce adherent polydopamine in a strong alkaline solution, which is a disadvantage for sensitive and accurate detection of DA [40–42]. Therefore, weak alkali condition (pH 9.0) was selected for this sensing system.

From Fig. S2, we can also see that the reaction time had remarkable influences on the RF intensity. In the first 30 min, RF intensities increased dramatically; after 30 min, only slight increases were observed. To achieve a quick response, 30 min of pre-incubation time was used for further experiments.

The effect of ionic strength on performance of this assay was investigated at pH 9 with five different buffer solutions: 100 mM PBS, 50 mM PBS, 10 mM PBS, 10 mM carbonate-bicarbonate buffer, and 10 mM boric acid-borate buffer. The results showed that the influence of different ions was insignificant.

The dose response of the sensing system towards DA was examined in detail under the optimized experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 , the RF intensity had different performances with various concentration ranges of DA. RF intensities vs. DA concentration were linear over the range 5–80 nM with a calibration equation of I420/I610 = 0.12C + 8.9 (C in nM, r = 0.9968), and 100–180 nM with a calibration equation of I420/I610 = 0.3C − 10.4 (C in nM, r = 0.9974). The detection limit of 2.9 nM was obtained (S/N = 3). We think that the different linear response at two concentration ranges is due to the different adsorption rate between DA and AuNCs.

The selectivity of the method was investigated by observing potential interferences of other molecules such as amino acids (including Ala, Arg, Asp, Cys, Gln, Glu, His, Ile, Leu, Lys, Trp, and Tyr), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), inositol (Ins), glucose (Glc), ions (including K+, Na+, Mg2+, and Ca2+) and other catecholamines and neurotransmitters including acetylcholine (ACH), serotonin, GABA, and norephinephrine (NE). As clearly shown in Fig. 6 , these competitors did not interfere with the results of the proposed method even if they were present at 100-fold higher concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers at the local hospital and diluted 10-fold with PBS (pH 9) before sensing. The diluted human serums were spiked with known concentrations of DA and measured under the optimum conditions. As summarized in Table 1 , the results obtained were in good agreement with the spiked values. These results suggest that matrix effects from the diluted serum did not affect the substantial detection.

In summary, a dual-emission RF sensor based on the hybridization of CDots and AuNCs was designed for the detection of DA. DA could quench the fluorescence of AuNCs and inhibit the FRET process between CDots and AuNCs with RF intensity change. With this strategy, DA could be specifically detected within 30 min over a range from 5 to 180 nM with a detection limit of 2.9 nM. Moreover, the RF probe was successfully applied to real serum samples, which validated the efficiency of the proposed probe for the determination of DA in complex samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Nature Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20140577), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Advanced Talents Science Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.03.080.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward the development of alpha(1a) adrenergic receptor antagonists
A nonoxidative sensor based on a self-doped polyaniline/carbon nanotube composite for sensitive and selective detection of the neurotransmitter dopamine
Selective detection of dopamine in the presence of ascorbic acid via fluorescence quenching of InP/ZnS quantum dots
Review of recent advances in analytical techniques for the determination of neurotransmitters
Quantitative determination of free and total dopamine in human plasma by LC-MS/MS: the importance of sample preparation
Studies on L-histidine capped Ag and Au nanoparticles for dopamine detection
Electrochemical sensor for dopamine based on a novel graphene-molecular imprinted polymers composite recognition element
Hydrogen generation from weak acids: electrochemical and computational studies of a diiron hydrogenase mimic
Detection of dopamine based on tyrosinase-Fe3O4 nanoparticles-chitosan nanocomposite biosensor
Sensitive determination of dopamine by its fluorescence enhancement on terbium(III)-dipicolinic acid system
Cu2+ modulated BSA–Au nanoclusters: a versatile fluorescence turn-on sensor for dopamine
Synthesizing a nano-composite of BSA-capped Au nanoclusters/graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets as a new fluorescent probe for dopamine detection
Turn-on near-infrared fluorescent sensor for selectively imaging serotonin
Selective catecholamine recognition with NeuroSensor 521: a fluorescent sensor for the visualization of norepinephrine in fixed and live cells
Neurotransmitter detection using corona phase molecular recognition on fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube sensors
Dual near-infrared two-photon microscopy for deep-tissue dopamine nanosensor imaging
A highly selective fluorescent probe for cadmium ions in aqueous solution and living cells
Click synthesis of a novel triazole bridged AIE active cyclodextrin probe for specific detection of Cd2+
A ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing hydrogen peroxide based on a hemicyanine-naphthol fluorophore
A ratiometric fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercuric ion
FRET-based modified graphene quantum dots for direct trypsin quantification in urine
A pyrene-benzthiazolium conjugate portraying aggregation induced emission, a ratiometric detection and live cell visualization of HSO3
Quantitative generalized ratiometric fluorescence spectroscopy for turbid media based on probe encapsulated by biologically localized embedding
FRET spectral unmixing: a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for Hypochlorite
Fluorescent gold nanoclusters: recent advances in sensing and imaging
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster nanosatellite for ratiometric fluorescence probe and imaging for hydrogen peroxide in living cells
Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and l-ascorbic acid
Tunable carbon-dot-based dual-emission fluorescent nanohybrids for ratiometric optical thermometry in living cells
Dual-channel probe of carbon dots cooperating with gold nanoclusters employed for assaying multiple targets
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
High photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots with unique double wavelength fluorescence emission for cell imaging
Fluorescent gold nanoclusters for selective detection of dopamine in cerebrospinal fluid
Fluorescent biosensors for real-time tracking of post-translational modification dynamics
Graphene quantum dots as effective probes for label-free fluorescence detection of dopamine
Turn-on fluorescent dopamine sensing based on in situ formation of visible light emitting polydopamine nanoparticles
The pH-controlled nanoparticles size of polydopamine for anti-cancer drug delivery
Self-polymerization of dopamine as a versatile and robust technique to prepare polymer capsules

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yu-Sheng He is currently doing his MS work at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. He is currently working toward sensors based functional nanomaterials.
Chang-Gang Pan is currently doing his MS work at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. He is currently working toward functional nanomaterial.
Hai-Xia Cao is currently doing his MS work at Department of Pharmaceutics, Jiangsu University. She is currently working toward biosensors.
Min-Zhi Yue is currently doing her BS work at Department of Pharmaceutics, Jiangsu University. She is currently working toward biosensors.

Li Wang received her MS degree and Ph.D degree from ShaanXi Normal University. She is currently working at Department of Pharmaceutics, Jiangsu University. Her current research interests include nanobiosensor, nanofabrication, and electrochemical biosensors.
Guo-Xi Liang received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University. He is currently working at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. His research interest is nanomaterial.
